critique.summary: The article discusses the challenges posed by Rust's coherence and orphan rules on ecosystem development, particularly focusing on trait implementations and their restrictions. It explores the reasons behind these rules, their impact on library evolution, and various proposals to relax or remove them to improve flexibility and ecosystem growth.
mainPoints: Rust's coherence and orphan rules restrict trait implementations to prevent conflicts and ensure soundness, but they also hinder ecosystem evolution by making it difficult to replace foundational crates or introduce alternatives.
The orphan rules enforce that only traits or types defined in the current crate can have trait implementations, limiting downstream crates from implementing traits for foreign types.
Coherence is necessary to avoid issues like conflicting trait implementations that could cause unsound behavior, such as inconsistent hashing in HashMaps or type safety violations.

Several proposals exist to relax or work around these rules, including binary crate exemptions, deferred coherence checks, coherence domains, and the introduction of incoherent traits with named impls and trait bound parameters.

Removing coherence entirely and adopting a model where trait implementations are explicit values passed around could solve ecosystem evolution problems but would require significant language and compiler changes.
Despite these challenges, coherence has been valuable for Rust's safety and soundness, and any changes must carefully balance flexibility with these guarantees.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the Rust language's coherence and orphan rules and their implications for trait implementations and ecosystem development. It references well-known Rust compiler errors, official Rust documentation, and existing discussions by Rust experts such as Niko Matsakis. The technical explanations and examples align with publicly available Rust language specifications and community knowledge. The proposals and their pros and cons are consistent with ongoing Rust language design discussions. Therefore, the claims made are truthful and well-founded based on current Rust language understanding.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore the practical impact of these rules on everyday Rust developers or provide empirical data on how often these limitations affect real-world projects.
It does not discuss the trade-offs between ecosystem flexibility and the guarantees of soundness and safety in detail, which are central to Rust's design philosophy.
The potential complexity and migration challenges of adopting incoherent traits or removing coherence are mentioned but not elaborated upon.
The article assumes familiarity with advanced Rust concepts and does not provide simplified explanations for less experienced readers.
It does not cover alternative approaches outside of Rust's trait system that might address serialization or interoperability challenges.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Rust's coherence and orphan rules, with some commenters defending these rules for ecosystem quality, while others criticize them for limiting flexibility and complicating dependency management. There is also debate about workarounds like wrapper types and the broader implications of open source package maintenance.

comments.author: ekidd
comments.text:
There's a well-known (and frequently encouraged) workaround for the orphan rule: Create a wrapper type.

Let's say you have one library with:

    pub struct TypeWithSomeSerialization { /* public fields here */ }
And you want to define a custom serialization. In this case, you can write:

    pub struct TypeWithDifferentSerialization(TypeWithSomeSerialization)
Then you just implement Serialize and Deserialize for TypeWithDifferentSerialization.

This cover most occasional cases where you need to work around the orphan rule. And semantically, it's pretty reasonable: If a type behaves differently, then it really isn't the same type.

The alternative is to have a situation where you have library A define a data type, library B define an interface, and library C implement the interface from B for the type from A. Very few languages actually allow this, because you run into the problem where library D tries to do the same thing library C did, but does it differently. There are workarounds, but they add complexity and confusion, which may not be worth it.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately explains a common and practical technique to work around the orphan rule in Rust, providing a clear and thoughtful perspective.

comments.author: Animats
comments.text:
Note the use case - someone wants to have the ability to replace a base-level crate such as serde.

When something near the bottom needs work, should there be a process for fixing it, which is a people problem? Or should there be a mechanism for bypassing it, which is a technical solution to a people problem? This is one of the curses of open source. The first approach means that there will be confrontations which must be resolved. The second means a proliferation of very similar packages.

This is part of the life cycle of an open source language. Early on, you don't have enough packages to get anything done, and are grateful that someone took the time to code something. Then it becomes clear that the early packages lacked something, and additional packages appear. Over time, you're drowning in cruft. In a previous posting, I mentioned ten years of getting a single standard ISO 8601 date parser adopted, instead of six packages with different bugs. Someone else went through the same exercise with Javascript.

Go tends to take the first approach, while Python takes the second. One of Go's strengths is that most of the core packages are maintained and used internally by Google. So you know they've been well-exercised.

Between Github and AI, it's all too easy to create minor variants of packages. Plus we now have package supply chain attacks. Curation has thus become more important. At this point in history, it's probably good to push towards the first approach.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view on the social and technical challenges in open source ecosystems, highlighting trade-offs relevant to Rust's package management.

comments.author: dathinab
comments.text:
This isn't a new discussion it was there around the early rust days too.

And IMHO coherence and orphan rules have majorly contributed to the quality of the eco system.
comments.analysis: Offers a valid historical perspective emphasizing the positive impact of these rules on Rust's ecosystem quality.

comments.author: nmilo
comments.text: I will never stop hating on the orphan rule, a perfect summary of what’s behind a lot of rust decisions. Purism and perfectionism at the cost of making a useful language, no better way to torpedo your ecosystem and make adding dependencies really annoying for no reason. Like not even a —dangerously-disable-the-orphan-rule, just no concessions here.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strongly negative opinion that is common among some Rust users, though somewhat subjective and lacking constructive alternatives.

comments.author: nixpulvis
comments.text:
I don't think explicit naming of impls is wise. They will regularly be TraitImpl or similar and add no real value. If you want to distinguish traits, perhaps force them to be within separate modules and use mod_a::mod_b::<Trait for Type> syntax.

> An interesting outcome of removing coherence and having trait bound parameters is that there becomes a meaningful difference between having a trait bound on an impl or on a struct:

This seems unfortunate to me.
comments.analysis: Raises a reasonable design critique about trait implementation naming and coherence, though more technical detail would strengthen the argument.

comments.author: grougnax
comments.text: If you think Rust has problems, it is that you've have not understood well Rust.
comments.analysis: Dismissive comment that adds little substantive value and does not engage with the discussion meaningfully.

comments.author: kelnos
comments.analysis: No content provided, thus no contribution to the discussion.
